docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Welcome to the StarUI Wiki!

StarUI is a developer-only resource that runs as a plugin. If you are a server owner, please just download StarUI and drop it into the plugins folder. However, there is a command, however, most of it is just for developers and developing the plugin itself. If you want to take a look at it, you can find it here Command

If you are a developer, please see Installation to get started

docs.title: The Command
docs.text: This command is intended as a maintainer and dependent plugin based command and has little to no use for a server owner. However, this page does document what the commands do as well as their permissions. The main permission is starui.admin

docs.title: /starui help
docs.text: Permission: starui.admin.help Prints out the command and sub-command usage to the one that used the command.

docs.title: /starui debug
docs.text: Permission: starui.admin.debug This command toggles debug mode, which all this does is print out most event information to the console. This is mostly used as an internal feature for creating and maintaining this plugin, however it may have some use to developers.

docs.title: /starui testgui
docs.text: Permission: starui.admin.testgui This command creates a test gui, where it has a normal element that does nothing that is a Diamond and an iron ingot button that prints out "You click the button". As more features get added to this GUI, there may bemore parameters to this command as well as more elements added to the test gui. This is mainly an internal development command, however it can be used by developers to see if the core of StarUI is working.

docs.title: /starui listguis
docs.text: Permission: starui.admin.listguis This command just lists out all of the open GUIs available to the GuiManager instance within StarUI. There isn't a while lot of information, but I do plan on adding sub commands to this one as well as doing some more stuff internally.

docs.title: Creating Simple Guis
docs.text: At it's core, StarUI is pretty simple, as it doesn't provide many high level features for UIs, however where it is simple, it allows a lot of flexibility with how you can interact with them. There are plans to add some more features, however these will not override the exisiting simplicity, so you can always use the bare-bones stuff to full customizability.

docs.title: InventoryGUI
docs.text: This class is a default implementation for the InventoryHandler interface and if you want simple GUis, this is your entry point.

docs.title: Creation
docs.text:
First, you call the constructor and pass in how many rows (Max of 6) and the Title of the GUI. The title can use color codes, however only default Spigot colors are supported. I plan on adding support for StarMCLib's ColorUtils, but that will be later. 

InventoryGUI gui = new InventoryGUI(6, "Test GUI");

docs.title: Adding Elements
docs.text:
Elements are the base for everything within this library and this is done to allow expandability. 

gui.setElement(0, 0, new Element().iconCreator(player -> new ItemStack(Material.DIAMOND)));
Now, you may be asking, what is with that iconCreator? Well, this is to allow more dynamic creation of items and usage of other item building libraries. I do plan on adding some factory methods to Element to make this a bit easier, but I have been using this in another project and it has come in extremely handy. Although, this library came from that project. The setElement method takes in a row, column and element. Rows and columns start at zero. There is an addElement method which looks for the first empty slot and sets it there. You can also set an element using the index. This is calculated using row * 9 + column.

docs.title: Removing Elements
docs.text: Removal of elements is done by a row and column

docs.title: Buttons
docs.text: Buttons allows you specify what happens when a player interacts with the item in the GUI. Buttons are a sub-class of Element and have some additional things. Buttons are added via the addElement, and setElement methods just like regular elements.

docs.title: Event  Consumer
docs.text: This is a Consumer that gives you the InventoryClickEvent. This is safe to assume that it is only within the GUI as there are several checks for that before this is called.

docs.title: Click Sound and Pitch
docs.text: In addition, you can also provide a Sound and a pitch for when the button is clicked. These are methods similar to the iconCreator method whereas they are builder-pattern.

docs.title: Filler Element
docs.text: This is a special element where you can add it as a filler, and if you call addElement, the method will treat it as if it is empty

docs.title: GuiManager Class
docs.text: This class is the entry point into StarUI, regardless of which approch you are using for how the library is loaded.

docs.title: Usage as a Plugin
docs.text:
When using StarUI as a stand-alone plugin, getting and instance is pretty easy. 

GuiManager guiManager = Bukkit.getServer().getServicesManager().getRegistration(GuiManager.class).getProvider();
Note: It is recommended to have a null check before calling getProvider, but for the sake of readability, I didn't do that here. Alternatively you can use the instanceof StarUI to get it. 
StarUI starUiPlugin = (StarUI) Bukkit.getServer().getPluginManager().getPlugin("StarUI");
GuiManager guiManager = starUiPlugin.getGuiManager();
Again, you should do a null check on the starUiPlugin instance before calling getGuiManager. The second method is not recommended though and either one returns the same instance.

docs.title: Usage as a Shaded Library
docs.text:
Getting a GuiManager instance is pretty simple as a shaded library, you just need to call the constructor, passing in your plugin. 

GuiManager guiManager = new GuiManager(plugin);
Then all you do is call the setup method to register things. 
guiManager.setup();
And you are good to go.

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text: Now you can move on to Creating GUIs. This is the same for both methods now that you have a GuiManager instance.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: StarUI runs as a plugin and is only intended to be run as a plugin. StarUI has no dependencies other than Spigot. However, there are two ways to use this library, as a plugin and as a shaded library. Dependency information is nearly the same, except you have to change some slight things.

docs.title: Choosing a Build Tool
docs.text: The two primary build tools for Java are Maven and Gradle. Pick one and stick with it.

docs.title: JitPack Repository
docs.text: Star Development LLC uses JitPack as the repository for development. You must have this repository in whichever build tool you wish to use.

docs.title: Maven
docs.text:
<repositories>
    <repository>
        <id>jitpack.io</id>
        <url>https://jitpack.io</url>
    </repository>
</repositories>

docs.title: Gradle
docs.text:
repositories {
    maven { url 'https://jitpack.io' }
}

docs.title: StarUI Dependency Information
docs.text: Replace {VERSION} with the version of StarUI you are using!

docs.title: Maven
docs.text:
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.github.StarDevelopmentLLC</groupId>
    <artifactId>StarUI</artifactId>
    <version>{VERSION}</version>
    <scope>provided</scope>
</dependency>
If you are using StarUI as a shaded library, make sure you add the maven shade plugin and change the scope to compile or just remove the scope option.

docs.title: Gradle
docs.text:
dependencies {
    compileOnly 'com.github.StarDevelopmentLLC:StarUI:{VERSION}'
}
If you are using StarUI as a shaded library, make sure you add the shadow Gradle plugin and change compileOnly to implementation

After you are done installing StarUI, take a look at GuiManager to see the entry point into the library.
